summary: DHH argues against the 'bring your whole self to work' movement, contending that employees should maintain a professional 'work self' that is genuine but bounded. He suggests the demand to bring one's whole self to work is a consequence of excessive work hours colonizing personal life. The work self should be courteous, engaged, and appropriately reserved — sharing no more than you would with a kind stranger on a long flight. He notes that deeper friendships can still form at work, but they're special precisely because they develop beyond professional obligations. The blurring of work and personal life through shared devices and always-on culture has eroded healthy boundaries that previous generations understood instinctively.
key_points: The 'whole self at work' demand is a bad trade that stems from employers expecting 60-80+ hour weeks
A 'work self' isn't a facade — it's a genuine but bounded version of you, like what you'd share with a kind stranger on a plane
Workplace friendships are more meaningful precisely because they develop beyond the professional context, not because work demanded full vulnerability
Holding something back creates a smaller surface area for interpersonal friction and preserves emotional reserves
The collapse of boundaries between work and personal devices mirrors the collapse of boundaries between work and personal identity
money_quotes: What work and you really need is for everyone to show up with their "work self".
Your work self needn't be a facade, it can still be you — just not all of it.
No more of your outside-of-work self than you'd be comfortable sharing with a kind stranger on a long plane ride.
Your whole self is a vulnerable show. Far more easily rattled, disturbed, or offended than the much slimmer slice that work is owed and due.
Holding some of yourself back means having something in reserve. Not putting it all in play.
A smaller surface area to rub others the wrong way and vice versa.
Work isn't owed all of you. Don't offer it.
key_insight: The 'bring your whole self to work' ethos is a bad deal for everyone — maintaining a bounded but genuine professional self preserves emotional reserves and healthier workplace dynamics.
